subject: Chemistry
solution:
The balanced chemical equation for the reaction between BaCl2 and Na3PO4 is:

3BaCl2 + 2Na3PO4 → Ba3(PO4)2 + 6NaCl

5 moles of barium chloride will require 5×23=3.3 moles of sodium phosphate.

Here, sodium phosphate is the limiting reagent.
2 moles of sodium phosphate produce 1 mole of barium phosphate.

Thus,2 moles of sodium phosphate will produce 1 mole of barium phosphate.

Hence, the maximum no. of moles of Ba3(PO4)2 that can be formed is one mole.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$74.75 \%$ of chlorine means $74.75 \mathrm{~g}$ chlorine is present in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of metal chloride.
Weight of metal $=100 \mathrm{~g}-74.75 \mathrm{~g}$ $=25.25 \mathrm{~g}$
Equivalent weight
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\text { weight of metal }}{\text { weight of chlorine }} \times 35.5 \\
& =\frac{25.25}{74.75} \times 35.5=12
\end{aligned}
$$
Valency of metal
$$
\begin{array}{r}
=\frac{2 \times \text { V.D. }}{\text { Equivalent wt. of metal }+35.5} \\
=\frac{2 \times 94.8}{12+35.5}=4
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Formula of compound $=\mathrm{MCl}_4$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
CH4+   2O2⟶   CO2+2H2O

10016    20832

Number of molesstoichiometric coefficient for CH4=6.251  Number of molesstoichiometric coefficient for O2=6.52=3.25

The reagent with lower ratio of number of moles to stoichiometric coefficient is called limiting reagent.

Hence, O2 is limiting reagent

Limiting reagent decides the amount of product,

As per the equation 2 moles of O2 gives rise to 1 mole of CO2, So 6.5 moles of O2 gives 6.52 moles of CO2

⇒nCO2=6.52

So mass of CO2=6.52×44=143gm

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Moles of ' C ' \(=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{1.46}{44}=0.033\)
Moles of ' C ' \(=\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{C}}=0.033 \times 12\)
Moles of 'H' \(=2 \times \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=2 \times \frac{0.567}{18}=0.063\)
Mass of ' H ' \(=0.0063\)
Mass of Oxygen \((\mathrm{O})=1-\left(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{H}}\right)\) \(=1-(0.033 \times 12+0.063 \times 1)=0.541 \mathrm{gm}\)
Moles of ' O ' \(=\frac{0.541}{16}=0.033\)
Empirical formula \(=\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{O}\)
Empirical formula mass \(=30\).

subject: Chemistry
solution:
In an unknown compounds containing $\mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{H}$
given $\%$ of $\mathrm{H}=12.5 \%$
$$
\therefore \% \text { of } N=100-12.5=87.5 \%
$$

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline \text { Element } & \text { Percentage } & \text { Atomic ratio } & \text { Simple ratio } \\
\hline \mathrm{H} & 12.5 \% & \frac{12.5}{1}=12.5 & \frac{12.5}{6.25}=2 \\
\hline \mathrm{N} & 87.5 & \frac{87.5}{14}=6.25 & \frac{6.25}{6.25}=1 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$2 \times$ vapour density $=$ Mol. wt $=16 \times 2=32$.
Molecular formula $=n \times$ empirical formula mass
$$
n=\frac{32}{16}=2
$$
$\therefore$ Molecular formula of the compound will be $=\left(\mathrm{NH}_2\right)_2$ $=\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{H}_4$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}$
Stoichoimetry ratio is $1: 2$
AT STP, $P=1 \mathrm{~atm}, T=273 \mathrm{~K}, R=0.0821$
Initial moles of $\mathrm{CO}_2 ; n\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right.$ initial $)=\frac{P V}{R T}$ $=\frac{1 \times 0.5}{0.0821 \times 273}=0.022 \mathrm{~mole}$
In final mixture no. of moles; $n\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}\right.$ mixture)
$$
=\frac{1 \times 0.7}{0.0821 \times 273}=0.031
$$
Increase in volume is by $=0.031-0.022$ $=0.009$ mole of gas
Final no. of moles of $\mathrm{CO}$ i.e. $n_{\text {(CO final) }}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=2 n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 \text { initial }\right)}-n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right. \text { final) }} \\
& \quad=2\left(0.022-\mathrm{n}_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 \text { final }\right)}\right. \\
& \left.n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-2 n_{\text {(CO }} \text { final }\right) \\
& \therefore \text { Now, } n_{\text {(CO final) }}+n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.031 \\
& n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right. \text { final) }}=0.031-n_{\text {(CO final) })} \ldots \text { (iii) }
\end{aligned}
$$
Substituting (ii) in eq. (i)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-2\left[0.031-n_{\text {(CO final) }}\right. \\
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-0.062+2 n_{\text {(CO final })}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
n_{(\mathrm{CO} \text { final) }}=0.018 \mathrm{~mol} .
$$
Volume of $\mathrm{CO}=V=\frac{n R T}{P}=\frac{0.018 \times 0.0821 \times 273}{1}$ $=0.40$ Litre
and volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2=0.7$ litre $-0.4$ litre $=0.3$ litre
$$
\therefore \mathrm{CO}_2=300 \mathrm{~mL}, \mathrm{CO}=400 \mathrm{~mL}
$$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Mass of C: Mass of H=6 : 1.
Or, for every atom of C (12 u), we have 2 atoms of H (1u × 2=2u)
Thus, the ratio of atoms of C : H=x : y=1 : 2
Thus, the formula is: CxH2xOz
Combustion of CxH2x:
CxH2x+(3x/2)O2→xCO2+xH2O
Hence, the combustion of CxH2x requires (3x/2) oxygen molecules.

Hence, the original molecule contains (3x/4) molecules =(3x/2) atoms.
Or, z=3x/2

Empirical Formula=CxH2xO3x/2=C2xH4xO3x=C2H4O3
